qa_pairs.question: According to the slide shown during the lecture, what is the title of the overall course lecture series being presented, and how does the direct-mapped cache address structure shown in the diagram divide a 32-bit address?
qa_pairs.answer: The lecture is part of MIT 6.004 Fall 2018, specifically Lecture 14 titled 'Caches 2,' presented by Silvina Hanono Wachman from CSAIL at MIT. The direct-mapped cache diagram shown divides the 32-bit address into three portions: the tag, the index, and the offset. The index uses K bits to select which of the 2^K cache lines to access, and the offset bits (specifically bits B-1 down to 2) select which word within the cache line, with the bottom two bits always zero for word alignment.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption identifying the lecture as MIT 6.004 Fall 2018 L14 Caches 2 by Silvina Hanono Wachman
Transcript and diagram describing how the 32-bit address is split into tag, index, and offset for direct-mapped cache

qa_pairs.question: The slide diagram for the n-way set-associative cache shows a specific structural difference from direct-mapped cache. Combining what the diagram reveals with the lecture explanation, what happens when the index bits are used in an n-way set-associative cache and why is it guaranteed that a hit cannot occur in more than one way simultaneously?
qa_pairs.answer: In the n-way set-associative cache diagram, the index bits select a set, which corresponds to one line in each of the n ways simultaneously. The tag is then compared against the tags stored in all n ways at that index. A hit in more than one way is impossible because the cache is designed so that a particular address only lives in one way at a time — storing the same address in multiple ways would waste space and the cache management policy ensures each address maps to exactly one way, so at most one way can contain a matching valid tag for any given address.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing the n-way set-associative cache diagram with Tag, Index, and Offset fields
Transcript explaining that index bits select a set and tag is compared in both ways, with guarantee of at most one hit

qa_pairs.question: The Cache Organization slide visually shows the internal components of the cache array unit including specific queues. Combining what the diagram shows with the lecture's description, what are the three SRAM components inside the cache array unit and what queues are used to communicate with the processor and back-end memory?
qa_pairs.answer: According to the Cache Organization diagram and the lecture explanation, the cache array unit contains three SRAMs: one for data (dataArray), one for the tag (tagArray), and one for the status (statusArray). For communication, there are queues on both sides: on the memory back-end side there is a lineReqQ (line request queue) for sending requests to DRAM and a lineRespQ (line response queue) for receiving responses from DRAM, and on the processor side there is a hitQ (hit queue) where data ready for the processor is placed. Additionally, there are registers for storing the current request and the current status.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption of Cache Organization diagram showing cache array unit with data, tag, status arrays and lineReqQ, lineRespQ, hitQ queues
Transcript describing the three SRAMs (data, tag, status) and the queues for line request, line response, and hit queue

qa_pairs.question: According to the frame captions describing the 'Stores' slide shown during this lecture, what are the two write policies described for handling a write hit, and what additional three-state status system is required specifically for the write-back policy?
qa_pairs.answer: The 'Stores' slide shows two write policies for handling a write hit: the write-back policy, which writes only to the cache and updates the next level memory when the line is evicted, and the write-through policy, which writes to both the cache and the next level memory simultaneously. The write-back policy specifically requires a three-state status system rather than just a valid bit, where the three states are: Invalid (data not present), Clean (valid data that has not been modified), and Dirty (valid data that has been modified and is inconsistent with main memory).

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame captions of Stores slide describing write-back and write-through policies and the Invalid/Clean/Dirty status states required for write-back

qa_pairs.question: The lecture explains the write-back policy and the write-miss allocate scheme together. Connecting the explanation of what write-back means with the explanation of what write-miss allocate means, what happens end-to-end when a processor executes a store to an address that is not currently in the cache?
qa_pairs.answer: When a store results in a cache miss with a write-back and write-miss allocate policy, the following occurs end-to-end: first, the cache checks its cache array unit and detects a miss; it then fetches the entire cache line containing the target address from main memory (DRAM) — this is the 'write-miss allocate' part, meaning the line is brought into the cache rather than writing directly to main memory. Once the line is in the cache, the processor's store data is written into the appropriate word location within that cached line. The cache line status is then marked dirty because it has been modified. The updated line is only written back to main memory later when that line is eventually evicted from the cache — this is the 'write-back' part.

ground_truth_spans.description: Transcript explaining write-back policy saves bandwidth by writing to main memory only on eviction
Transcript explaining write-miss allocate brings the line into cache on a miss rather than writing directly to main memory

qa_pairs.question: In the context of the cache array unit (CAU) response types described in this lecture, what are the three possible outcomes that can be returned by the CAU's response method, and what data is returned in each case?
qa_pairs.answer: The cache array unit response (CAUResp) can indicate three possible outcomes via the hitMiss enumeration field. First, a load hit (LdHit): the CAU returns the specific word that was requested, provided in the loadValue parameter of the response. Second, a store hit (StHit): the CAU does not need to return any data to the processor since the write was applied directly to the cache line in the CAU. Third, a miss: the CAU returns the entire evicted cache line in the tagLine parameter, so that if the line was dirty it can be written back to main memory before the new line is fetched.

ground_truth_spans.description: Transcript describing the three CAU response types: LdHit returning loadValue word, StHit returning nothing, and Miss returning the entire evicted line

qa_pairs.question: When constructing the line address for a DRAM request during a cache miss in the blocking cache implementation described in this lecture, exactly which bits from the original processor request are used and how are they combined?
qa_pairs.answer: When making a line request to DRAM during a cache miss, the line address is constructed by taking only the tag bits and the index bits from the original processor's 32-bit address, and concatenating them together. The offset bits are excluded because a DRAM line request fetches an entire cache line (16 words, 64 bytes), so the specific byte or word offset within the line is not needed for addressing in DRAM — the line address identifies which full line to fetch. This concatenation of tag and index bits from the currReq register forms the complete line address sent in the line request.

ground_truth_spans.description: Transcript explaining that the line address for DRAM is formed by concatenating tag bits and index bits from the original request, omitting the offset bits

qa_pairs.question: The slide titled 'Replacement Policy' is shown visually. What does it say about replacement choices for a direct-mapped cache, and how does the transcript explain the LRU algorithm's motivation for set-associative caches?
qa_pairs.answer: The 'Replacement Policy' slide states that for a direct-mapped cache there is no choice because each address maps to exactly one cache line. For set-associative caches, the transcript explains that Least Recently Used (LRU) is the most common algorithm, motivated by the locality principle: if something was used recently it is likely to be used again, so evicting the least recently used item minimizes future misses.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption of Replacement Policy slide stating direct-mapped cache has no choice
Transcript explaining LRU motivation based on locality principle

qa_pairs.question: The lecture slide shown visually is titled 'Blocking vs. Non-Blocking cache.' What properties does each type have according to the slide caption, and what does the transcript say about which type will be implemented in the class?
qa_pairs.answer: According to the slide caption, a blocking cache has at most one outstanding miss, must wait for memory to respond, and does not accept processor requests in the meantime. A non-blocking cache can handle multiple outstanding requests simultaneously. The transcript states that non-blocking caches are much more complex, so the class will focus on and implement blocking caches.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption listing blocking cache properties: one outstanding miss, wait for memory, no new requests
Transcript stating non-blocking is more complex and class will focus on blocking caches

qa_pairs.answer: The 'Cached Memory Systems' slide diagram shows a processor with a register file (rf) and program counter (pc) connected to an SRAM-based cache, which in turn is connected to a DRAM-based memory via lineReq and lineResp signals. The transcript explains that processor requests are word requests providing a 32-bit address and expecting a 32-bit response, whereas SRAM requests to DRAM are line requests fetching 16 words (64 bytes) at a time.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption of Cached Memory Systems diagram showing processor, SRAM cache, DRAM memory, lineReq, lineResp
Transcript distinguishing 32-bit word requests from processor vs 16-word line requests from SRAM to DRAM

qa_pairs.question: A slide titled 'Cache Interface' is shown visually in the lecture. According to the frame caption, how many methods does the cache interface have and what are they, and what does the transcript say about which methods are processor-side versus back-end side?
qa_pairs.answer: According to the frame caption and transcript, the cache interface has four methods: req (request) and resp (response) on the processor side, and lineReq (line request) and lineResp (line response) on the back-end DRAM side. The transcript explains that the first two methods are how the processor communicates with the cache, while the other two are how the cache makes requests to and accepts responses from main memory.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption of Cache Interface slide showing req, resp, lineReq, lineResp
Transcript explaining processor-side methods vs back-end DRAM-side methods

qa_pairs.question: The frame caption of a slide in this lecture shows a 'Hit and miss performance' slide. What latency is described for a cache hit, and what two different miss latency formulas are given for the no-evacuation and evacuation cases?
qa_pairs.answer: According to the 'Hit and miss performance' slide, a cache hit has a 1-cycle latency with appropriate hitQ design, directly related to the latency of the L1 cache. For a miss with no evacuation (no dirty line to write back), the latency is DRAM load latency plus 2 times SRAM latency. For a miss requiring evacuation (dirty line must be written back), the latency is DRAM store latency plus DRAM load latency plus 2 times SRAM latency.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing hit: 1-cycle latency with hitQ design
Frame caption showing miss no evacuation: DRAM load + 2x SRAM, and evacuation: DRAM store + DRAM load + 2x SRAM

qa_pairs.question: According to the visual slide caption for the 'Cache Organization' slide, what three arrays make up the cache-array unit shown in the diagram?
qa_pairs.answer: According to the frame caption for the 'Cache Organization' slide, the cache-array unit diagram shows three arrays: a Data Array (represented by yellow rectangles), a Tag Array (represented by orange rectangles), and a Status Array (represented by blue rectangles).

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption describing cache-array unit with data array, tag array, and status array

qa_pairs.question: The lecture explains the three possible states of a cache line needed to support write-back caches. What are those three states and what does each mean? Also, what is the Miss-allocate policy described later for handling write misses?
qa_pairs.answer: The three states for a cache line under a write-back policy are: Invalid (data is not valid), Clean (data is valid but has not been modified, so it is consistent with main memory), and Dirty (data is valid but has been modified, so it is not consistent with main memory). The Miss-allocate policy means that on a write miss, the cache always brings the missing line into the cache before performing the write, rather than writing directly to main memory.

ground_truth_spans.description: Transcript explaining invalid, clean, dirty states for write-back cache support
Transcript explaining miss-allocate policy: bring line into cache on write miss

qa_pairs.question: The lecture describes the DRAM interface and the SRAM interface separately. What fixed line size does the class assume for DRAM accesses, and how does the SRAM interface differ in that it allows the user to specify its size?
qa_pairs.answer: The class assumes a fixed DRAM line size of 64 bytes, which corresponds to 16 words fetched at a time from main memory. The SRAM interface differs because it allows the user to specify the number of index bits when instantiating it, which determines how many lines the cache holds (2 to the power of the number of index bits), and also allows specification of the data type it stores.

ground_truth_spans.description: Transcript stating DRAM fixed size is 64 bytes, 16 words fetched at a time
Transcript explaining SRAM allows specifying number of index bits to determine cache size

qa_pairs.question: In the lecture, what specific reason is given for why a 2-way set-associative cache can implement LRU replacement using just one bit of state per cache line?
qa_pairs.answer: The lecture states that for a 2-way set-associative cache, you can implement Least Recently Used replacement by adding just one bit of state to each of your cache lines. This single bit is sufficient because with only two ways, you only need to track which of the two ways was used most recently, which is a binary choice requiring exactly one bit.

ground_truth_spans.description: Transcript explaining 2-way set-associative LRU implemented with one bit per cache line

qa_pairs.question: The blackboard frame for the 2D version shows a grid with labels for rows and columns and defines a 2D peak condition. What does the professor say is the worst-case complexity of the greedy ascent algorithm for this problem, and what does it reduce to when the matrix dimensions are equal?
qa_pairs.answer: The blackboard shows the 2D version with an n-row by m-column matrix and defines a 2D peak using conditions a ≥ b, a ≥ c, a ≥ d, a ≥ e. The professor states that a greedy ascent algorithm for the 2D peak-finding problem has Θ(nm) complexity in the worst case, because one may end up touching a large fraction of all elements in the matrix. When N equals M, this reduces to Θ(n²) complexity, which is significantly worse.

qa_pairs.question: The lecture slide shown early in the lecture lists several topics to be covered. What sorting algorithm is described as a divide and conquer approach, and what specific technique is introduced to improve the comparison complexity of the simpler sorting algorithm discussed first?
qa_pairs.answer: Merge sort is the divide and conquer algorithm listed on the lecture slide. The simpler algorithm, insertion sort, can have its comparison complexity improved from Theta n squared to Theta n log n by using binary search to find the correct insertion position in the already-sorted portion of the array, rather than doing sequential pairwise comparisons.

qa_pairs.question: The recursion tree diagram on the blackboard shows nodes labeled Cn, Cn/2, Cn/4, etc. How many levels does this tree have and what is the total work summed across all levels, as derived from the diagram?
qa_pairs.answer: The recursion tree has 1 plus log n levels, where log is base 2. At each level the total work done is Cn, because the two halves of size n/2 each contribute Cn/2 and together sum back to Cn. Multiplying the work per level by the number of levels gives Cn times (1 plus log n), which equals Theta n log n, proving merge sort's overall complexity.

ground_truth_spans.description: Recursion tree diagram on blackboard showing Cn at root, Cn/2 at second level, Cn/4 at third level, with branches
Explanation that each level sums to Cn and there are 1+log n levels giving T(n) = Theta n log n

qa_pairs.question: The blackboard displays the recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + Cn^2. Using the recursion tree argument shown in the lecture, where does most of the work occur in this recurrence and what is its overall complexity?
qa_pairs.answer: In the recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + Cn^2, most of the work is done at the root, the top level of the recursion. The top level contributes Cn^2, while each subsequent level contributes a geometrically decreasing fraction: Cn^2/2 at the second level, Cn^2/4 at the third, and so on. The sum is bounded by 2Cn^2, so the overall complexity is Theta n squared, dominated by the work at the root.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard showing recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + Cn^2 with recursion tree nodes Cn^2, Cn^2/4, Cn^2/16
Explanation that top level dominates and the algorithm is Theta n squared with all work at the root

qa_pairs.question: The merge sort diagram on the blackboard shows an array A divided into parts L and R. The lecture explains that sorting actually happens in a specific subroutine. What is that subroutine called, and what property must its inputs satisfy according to the lecture?
qa_pairs.answer: The subroutine is called merge. Its inputs must be two sorted arrays, referred to as L prime and R prime, meaning that the left and right halves must already be sorted before the merge routine is invoked. The merge routine then combines these two sorted arrays into a single sorted output array using a two-finger algorithm that compares elements from each array in sequence.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard diagram showing array A divided into L and R with Sort L and Sort R sub-diagrams and merge step
Explanation that merge assumes two sorted arrays L prime and R prime as input, using the two-finger algorithm

qa_pairs.question: The blackboard shows the merge procedure working on two sorted arrays: [20, 13, 7, 2] and [12, 11, 9, 1]. What algorithm is used to merge them and what is the complexity of merging two arrays each of size n/2?
qa_pairs.answer: The two-finger algorithm is used to merge the two sorted arrays. One pointer tracks the current minimum of each input array, and at each step the smaller of the two pointed-to elements is written to the output array and the corresponding pointer advances. The complexity of merging two arrays each of size n/2 is Theta n overall, since every element is examined exactly once across both input arrays.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard showing two sorted arrays [20,13,7,2] and [12,11,9,1] labeled as L prime and R prime with two-finger algorithm notation

The crossover point where merge sort in Python beats insertion sort in C occurs at approximately n equals 4000, beyond which the Theta n log n asymptotic advantage outweighs the constant factor advantage of the compiled C implementation.

qa_pairs.question: The lecture explains why binary insertion sort does not achieve Theta n log n complexity for both comparisons and swaps when using an array data structure. What is the specific reason that swaps remain Theta n squared even after applying binary search?
qa_pairs.answer: Even though binary search reduces the number of comparisons to Theta n log n, the number of swaps remains Theta n squared because inserting element A[i] into its correct position in the already-sorted subarray A[0] through A[i-1] requires physically shifting all elements between the insertion point and position i-1 one step to the right. In the worst case this shift involves Theta n elements per insertion step, and with Theta n insertion steps the total swap count is Theta n squared regardless of how efficiently the correct position is found.

ground_truth_spans.description: Explanation that binary search gives Theta n log n comparisons by searching the sorted portion in log i time per step
Explanation that inserting into an array requires shifting elements, giving Theta n swaps per step and Theta n squared total

qa_pairs.question: The lecture discusses why sorting a list first makes finding the median easy, and also mentions binary search as another problem that benefits from a sorted list. What are the exact complexities for finding the median in a sorted list and for finding a specific element using binary search in a sorted list?
qa_pairs.answer: Finding the median in a sorted list is constant time, or O(1), because you simply look at the element at index n divided by 2 in the sorted array B, which by definition has an equal number of elements smaller and larger than it. Finding a specific element using binary search in a sorted list takes logarithmic time, O(log n), because at each step you compare the target to the middle element and eliminate half the remaining search space, converting what would be a linear scan into a logarithmic one.

ground_truth_spans.description: Explanation that finding the median is constant time by looking at B[n/2] in a sorted array
Explanation that binary search finds a specific element in logarithmic time in a sorted array

qa_pairs.question: The lecture introduces build-max-heap and max-heapify as the two main heap operations on the blackboard. What precondition must hold for max-heapify to run correctly, and why does the build-max-heap algorithm start from index n/2 rather than n?
qa_pairs.answer: The precondition for max-heapify(A, i) is that the subtrees rooted at left(i) and right(i) must already be max-heaps. Build-max-heap starts from index n/2 rather than n because elements from index n/2+1 through n are all leaves of the tree, and leaves trivially satisfy the max-heap property since they have no children to compare against. Starting at n/2 ensures the precondition is met for every call, as the children of each node processed are already valid max-heaps.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing 'Max-Heapify(A,i): Assume that the trees rooted at left(i) and right(i) are max-heaps'
Frame caption and transcript showing elements A[n/2+1..n] are all leaves, and build-max-heap starts at n/2

qa_pairs.question: The lecture's blackboard shows the Heap Sort algorithm steps and a small example with values 4, 2, 1. How does the swap-and-discard strategy shown in the diagram avoid needing a separate extract_max procedure, and what is the overall time complexity of Heap Sort?
qa_pairs.answer: The swap-and-discard strategy works by swapping the root element (the maximum) with the last element in the heap array, then decrementing the heap size to effectively discard the maximum from the heap without needing to physically remove it from the array. In the example shown, 4 is swapped with 1 to give array [1,2,4], then 4 is discarded by shrinking heap size, leaving [1,2] as the heap. Max-heapify is then called on the new root to restore the max-heap property. The overall time complexity of Heap Sort is O(n log n) because build-max-heap is O(n) but the n iterations of swap plus max-heapify each cost O(log n).

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing diagram of binary trees with values 4,2,1 and transformation steps in heap sort
Transcript explaining swap of top element with bottom element, discard by decrement, and overall O(n log n) complexity

To be a perfect (complete) binary tree, the array would need exactly 15 elements, which would fill all levels entirely. Because there are only 10 elements, the bottom level of the tree is not completely filled, making it a nearly complete binary tree rather than a perfect one.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing 'Heap: An array visualized as a nearly complete binary tree' and transcript explaining 10 elements cannot form a perfect binary tree requiring 15 elements

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing 'max-heapify: Correct a single violation of the heap property in a subtree's root' written on blackboard

qa_pairs.question: The lecture explains how build-max-heap uses max-heapify as a building block and why calling max-heapify in a specific order satisfies its precondition at every step. What is the calling order and why does it guarantee the precondition is always met?
qa_pairs.answer: Build-max-heap calls max-heapify for i from n/2 down to 1, processing nodes from the bottom of the tree upward toward the root. This order guarantees the precondition because when max-heapify is called on node i, it requires that the subtrees rooted at left(i) and right(i) are already max-heaps. Since the algorithm works bottom-up, by the time any non-leaf node is processed, all nodes below it have already been processed and their subtrees converted to valid max-heaps. Leaves (indices n/2+1 through n) are trivially max-heaps since they have no children.

ground_truth_spans.description: Transcript presenting build-max-heap pseudocode: for i = n/2 downto 1 do max-heapify(A,i)
Transcript explaining leaves are automatically max-heaps and bottom-up order satisfies precondition at each step

qa_pairs.question: According to the lecturer, what is the total time complexity of Heap Sort and why is it not dominated by the build-max-heap step?
qa_pairs.answer: The total time complexity of Heap Sort is O(n log n). Even though build-max-heap runs in O(n) time, the algorithm then repeatedly calls max-heapify n times (once for each element extracted), and each max-heapify call takes O(log n) time, giving O(n log n) for that repeated phase. The O(n) build step is dominated by the O(n log n) extraction phase.

qa_pairs.question: The lecture visually shows the amortization definition on the blackboard as 'operation takes T(n) amortized if k operations take ≤ k·T(n) time'. How does this definition apply to the table doubling insertion analysis, and what does this imply about the amortized cost per insertion?
qa_pairs.answer: The amortization definition states that if k operations in total take at most k times T(n), then each operation is said to take T(n) amortized. Applied to table doubling with insertions: k insertions using the doubling strategy incur a total cost that forms a geometric series summing to Theta(k), because rebuilds happen at sizes 1, 2, 4, 8, ..., k with each rebuild costing linear in the rebuild size, and these costs sum geometrically. Since k total insertions take Theta(k) total time, dividing by k gives Theta(1) amortized time per insertion, meaning each insert costs O(1) on average across all operations.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard shows amortization definition: operation takes T(n) amortized if k operations take ≤ k·T(n) time
Table doubling k inserts take Theta(k) total time implying Theta(1) amortized per insert

qa_pairs.question: The lecture slide titled 'Lecture 9: Table Doubling, Karp-Rabin' shown at the start introduces two major topics. How does the rolling hash ADT shown later on the blackboard — with operations r.append(c), r.skip(c), and r() — connect to implementing the Karp-Rabin algorithm for string matching?
qa_pairs.answer: The lecture slide at the start identifies the two main topics as table doubling and Karp-Rabin string matching. The rolling hash ADT shown later on the blackboard provides the key data structure for Karp-Rabin: r.append(c) adds a character to the end of the maintained string x, r.skip(c) deletes the first character of x assuming it is c, and r() returns the hash value h(x) of the current string. In Karp-Rabin, two rolling hashes rs and rt are created — rs stores the hash of the pattern s, and rt maintains the hash of the current window in t. By calling skip and append in each step, the window slides in O(1) time, enabling linear expected-time string matching by comparing hash values instead of full strings.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecture slide shown at start: '6.006, Fall 2011 Lecture 9: Table Doubling, Karp-Rabin'
Blackboard shows Rolling hash ADT with r.append(c), r.skip(c), r() operations and their use in Karp-Rabin algorithm

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows blackboard text defining Rolling hash ADT with r.append(c), r.skip(c), r maintains string x, r(): hash value of x=h(x)

qa_pairs.question: What does the lecture state about implementing all hash table operations in constant worst-case time per operation rather than constant amortized time, and what is the key insight behind the technique?
qa_pairs.answer: The lecture states that it is possible to achieve constant worst-case time per operation rather than merely constant amortized time, though it is described as tricky to implement and rarely done in practice because of its complexity. The key insight is that when you detect the table is getting full, instead of waiting until the table is completely full and then doing a linear-time rebuild all at once, you begin building a new table of twice the size on the side incrementally. Each time you insert into the actual table, you simultaneously move a small constant number of items — enough items so that by the time the old table is completely full, all items have already been moved to the new table, and you can switch over to the new table immediately.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecture describes constant worst-case time technique: build new table on side incrementally, moving a constant number of items per insert

What does the lecture say about how Python lists implement their dynamic resizing, and what amortized complexity do list.append and list.pop achieve?

The lecture explains that Python lists are implemented using arrays and use table doubling with some constant (possibly 2 or another constant) to support dynamic resizing. Both list.append and list.pop achieve Theta(1) amortized time, though in reality they are constant amortized rather than worst-case constant.

qa_pairs.question: The blackboard frame shows the Rolling hash ADT with operations r.append(c) and r.skip(c). How does the Karp-Rabin algorithm use two instances of this ADT, called rs and rt, to perform string matching?
qa_pairs.answer: The blackboard shows the Rolling hash ADT with append and skip operations. The Karp-Rabin algorithm creates two rolling hash instances: rs and rt. For rs, it appends every character of pattern s to obtain the hash of s. For rt, it appends the first len(s) characters of t. Then for each position in t, it checks if rs() equals rt(); if hashes match, it verifies character by character. It then advances by calling rt.skip(t[i-len(s)]) and rt.append(t[i]) to slide the window, achieving linear expected time overall.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows Rolling hash ADT with r.append(c) and r.skip(c) on blackboard
Lecture describes Karp-Rabin using rs and rt rolling hashes to compare and slide window

qa_pairs.question: What does the frame caption describe as the content of the blackboard slide titled 'Simple algorithm' for string matching?
qa_pairs.answer: The frame caption describes the blackboard as showing the simple string matching algorithm written as the Python one-liner: any(s == t[i:i+len(s)] for i in range(len(t)-len(s))), along with a diagram illustrating the sliding comparison of s against substrings of t of the same length.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows simple algorithm any(s==t[i:i+len(s)] for i in range(len(t)-len(s))) on blackboard

qa_pairs.question: Why must a new hash function be built when growing a hash table, and what is the cost of the grow-table operation in terms of n and m?
qa_pairs.answer: A new hash function must be built when growing because the hash function maps the universe of keys to a table of a specific size m; if m changes, the old hash function only uses the beginning of the table and new slots would never be used, so every key must be rehashed. The cost of the grow-table operation is order n plus m plus m-prime, because you pay order m to iterate over all slots, order n to visit all list entries, and order m-prime to initialize the new table; in the constructed scenario this is Theta(n).

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecture explains why new hash function needed: old function only maps to old table size
Lecture gives cost of grow-table as order n plus m plus m-prime, Theta(n)

qa_pairs.question: According to the lecture, what specific condition triggers a false positive in the Karp-Rabin algorithm, and what does the algorithm do when a hash match is detected?
qa_pairs.answer: A false positive in Karp-Rabin occurs because of hash collisions: two distinct strings may map to the same hash value, so equal hash values do not guarantee the strings themselves are equal. When a hash match is detected (rs() equals rt()), the algorithm checks the actual substring character by character by comparing s to t[i-len(s)+1:i+1] to verify a true match, spending order |s| time only when a potential match is found.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecture explains false positives from collisions and character-by-character verification when hash matches

qa_pairs.question: What visual diagram appears on the lecture slide introducing today's topics, and how does the DFS algorithm shown in the pseudocode handle vertices that have already been visited?
qa_pairs.answer: The introductory lecture slide shows a graph diagram with nodes and edges alongside the topic list including depth-first search, edge classification, cycle detection, and topological sort. The DFS algorithm handles already-visited vertices by checking if a vertex v is in the parent dictionary; if it already has a parent entry, the algorithm simply skips it and moves to the next edge, ensuring each vertex is visited at most once.

ground_truth_spans.description: Introductory slide showing today's topics and graph diagram visual
DFS-Visit pseudocode showing how already-visited vertices are skipped via parent check

qa_pairs.question: The lecture slide shows the top-level DFS pseudocode iterating over all vertices V, and later the blackboard displays 'Cycle detection: G has a cycle iff DFS has a back edge'. How does the top-level DFS loop contribute to ensuring cycle detection works for graphs that are not strongly connected?
qa_pairs.answer: The top-level DFS loop iterates over every vertex in V and calls DFS-Visit on any vertex that has not yet been visited. This ensures that all connected components of the graph are explored, not just the part reachable from a single starting vertex. For cycle detection, this means back edges in any component of the graph will be discovered, guaranteeing that if any directed cycle exists anywhere in the graph, it will be detected because the corresponding back edge will be found during the exhaustive traversal.

ground_truth_spans.description: Top-level DFS pseudocode shown iterating over all vertices to handle disconnected graphs
Cycle detection theorem stated: G has cycle iff DFS has back edge

qa_pairs.question: According to the frame caption describing the blackboard content during the analysis section, what is the exact time complexity stated for DFS and what two components does it break down into?
qa_pairs.answer: According to the frame caption showing the blackboard analysis, the exact time complexity stated for DFS is Theta(V+E), described as linear time. It breaks down into two components: O(V) for visiting each vertex once in the DFS outer loop, and O(E) for the work done inside DFS-Visit paying for each vertex's adjacency list entries, which sums to O(E) across all vertices by the handshaking lemma.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows 'Analysis: Theta(V+E) (linear time) - visit each vertex once in DFS alone O(V) - DFS-Visit called once per vertex'

qa_pairs.question: What does the frame caption of the topological sort section reveal about the relationship between DFS finishing times and the topological order output?
qa_pairs.answer: The frame caption of the topological sort section reveals that the algorithm runs DFS and outputs the reverse of the finishing times of vertices. Specifically, it states 'run DFS, output reverse of finishing time of vertices', meaning that as each vertex finishes its DFS-Visit recursion, it is added to a list, and this list is then reversed to produce the valid topological ordering where all edges point from lower-order to higher-order vertices.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows 'Topological sort: run DFS, output reverse of finishing time of vertices'

qa_pairs.question: The lecture explains how backward edges in DFS can be detected using an 'in-process' marking scheme, and also explains why a back edge guarantees a cycle exists. How does the in-process marking mechanism allow detection of backward edges, and what logical argument proves that a back edge implies a directed cycle?
qa_pairs.answer: The in-process marking works by setting a flag 'inprocess = true' at the start of DFS-Visit on a node and 'inprocess = false' at the end, tracking which nodes are currently on the recursion stack. When following an edge to a node that is already marked in-process, that node is an ancestor in the current recursion stack, so the edge is a backward edge. This implies a directed cycle because: the backward edge goes from the current node to its ancestor, and there is a chain of tree edges forming a path from that ancestor down to the current node, so combining the tree path with the backward edge forms a directed cycle.

ground_truth_spans.description: In-process marking scheme: set inprocess=true at start and inprocess=false at end of DFS-Visit to track recursion stack
Proof that back edge implies cycle: tree edges form path from ancestor to descendant, back edge completes the directed cycle

qa_pairs.question: The lecture discusses why BFS is preferred for shortest paths while DFS is not, and also describes what DFS can uniquely do that BFS cannot do as efficiently. What specific limitation of BFS regarding graph exploration is mentioned, and what capability of DFS makes it useful for graphs that are not strongly connected?
qa_pairs.answer: The limitation of BFS mentioned is that it only explores vertices reachable from a single source vertex s; if a vertex cannot be reached from s, BFS gives infinite distance and cannot explore that component. DFS overcomes this with the top-level outer loop that iterates over all vertices in V and calls DFS-Visit on any unvisited vertex, effectively restarting the search at each new connected component or strongly connected component, allowing it to explore the entire graph regardless of connectivity and find all different clusters.

ground_truth_spans.description: BFS explores layer by layer and detects unreachable vertices, DFS used to explore whole graph not just part reachable from s
DFS outer loop iterates over all vertices to find all clusters and strongly connected components

qa_pairs.question: According to the lecture, what is the specific mathematical relationship used to justify that the sum of all adjacency list lengths across all vertices equals E for directed graphs, and what is the name given to this relationship?
qa_pairs.answer: The lecture states that the sum over all vertices V of the length of the adjacency list for each vertex equals E for directed graphs, or 2E for undirected graphs. This relationship is called the Handshaking Lemma, which was introduced in the previous lecture. It is used to justify that the total work done in the DFS-Visit for-loops across all recursive calls is O(E), completing the O(V+E) linear time analysis.

ground_truth_spans.description: Handshaking Lemma mentioned: sum of adjacency list lengths is E for directed graphs, 2E for undirected, from last lecture

qa_pairs.question: The lecture describes how the textbook records timestamps during DFS to distinguish forward edges from cross edges. What two specific timestamps are recorded for each vertex, and how are they described in terms of the algorithm's execution?
qa_pairs.answer: The textbook records two timestamps for each vertex: a start time recorded when DFS begins visiting that vertex, and a finish time recorded when DFS completes visiting that vertex and all vertices reachable from it. These are described as being measured by incrementing a counter every time any step in the algorithm occurs, so the start time is the counter value when DFS-Visit begins on the vertex and the finish time is the counter value when DFS-Visit returns from the vertex.

ground_truth_spans.description: Textbook approach: record start time when vertex begins being visited and finish time when done, using incrementing counter

qa_pairs.question: The board shows an edge classification listing tree edges, forward edges, backward edges, and cross edges. What specific structural property distinguishes a forward edge from a backward edge according to the instructor's explanation?
qa_pairs.answer: A forward edge goes from a node in the tree to a descendant of that node, meaning there is already a tree path from the source to the destination. A backward edge goes from a node to one of its ancestors in the tree, meaning the destination is higher up in the tree and was visited before the source node.

Instructor explains backward edge goes from node to ancestor in tree

qa_pairs.question: The board shows the DFS-Visit pseudocode. According to the instructor's explanation of the example traversal, when DFS starts from vertex A and follows the path A→B→E→D, what happens when DFS examines D's only outgoing edge?
qa_pairs.answer: When DFS examines D's only outgoing edge, which goes to B, it finds that B has already been visited and already has a parent pointer set. Therefore the algorithm skips that edge, doing nothing in the else case, and since there are no remaining outgoing edges from D, DFS finishes processing D and backtracks.

ground_truth_spans.description: Board shows DFS-Visit pseudocode with parent check
Instructor explains D's edge to B is skipped because B already has a parent pointer

qa_pairs.question: How does the instructor explain that the DFS-Visit function is called at most once per vertex, and what term does he use to describe the total cost of all adjacency list iterations across all DFS-Visit calls?
qa_pairs.answer: The instructor explains that DFS-Visit is called at most once per vertex because immediately before calling DFS-Visit on vertex v, its parent is set for the first time, meaning it was not in the parent structure before, so it can never be called again. The total cost of iterating over all adjacency lists across all DFS-Visit calls is order of the sum of adjacency list lengths over all vertices, which equals E, the number of edges, by the handshaking lemma.

ground_truth_spans.description: Instructor explains DFS-Visit called once per vertex because parent is set right before the call
Instructor says total edge traversal cost equals sum of adjacency list lengths which is E

qa_pairs.question: What specific method does the instructor describe for detecting backward edges during DFS, and what marking is placed on vertices to enable this detection?
qa_pairs.answer: The instructor describes marking each vertex with an 'in process' flag. At the beginning of DFS-Visit on a vertex s, a flag s.inprocess is set to true, and at the end of visiting s it is set back to false. When following an edge to a neighbor, if that neighbor's inprocess flag is true, it means the neighbor is currently on the recursion stack and is an ancestor of the current node, so the edge is classified as a backward edge.

ground_truth_spans.description: Instructor explains marking nodes with inprocess flag at start and end of DFS-Visit to detect back edges

qa_pairs.question: In the DFS traversal example starting from vertex C as the second source, what happens when DFS from C visits vertex F and then examines F's only outgoing edge?
qa_pairs.answer: When DFS from C visits vertex F, it sets F's parent pointer to C. Then DFS examines F's only outgoing edge, which goes back to F itself. Since F already has a parent pointer set, it has already been visited, so the algorithm skips that edge and does nothing. There are no remaining outgoing edges from F, so DFS finishes with F and backtracks to C.

ground_truth_spans.description: Instructor describes DFS from C visiting F and finding F's edge leads back to already-visited F

qa_pairs.question: Why does a direct access array fail as a practical data structure when keys are MIT student IDs, and what variable does the professor introduce to describe this problem formally?
qa_pairs.answer: A direct access array fails as a practical data structure for MIT student IDs because those IDs are nine-digit numbers, meaning they can be as large as 10^9. To use a direct access array indexed by key value, one must allocate an array large enough to span the entire range of possible key values, which would be approximately 10^9 entries. However, the actual number of students in the class is only about 300 to 400, so the array would be allocated for roughly a billion entries while only a few hundred are used, wasting enormous amounts of space and making order operations like finding the next element very slow. The professor formally introduces the variable u to represent the maximum size of any key—equivalently, the size of the universe of possible keys—to describe this mismatch between n (number of items stored) and u (size of key space).

ground_truth_spans.description: Explanation that MIT IDs are nine-digit numbers requiring array of size 10^9
Introduction of variable u as the maximum key size representing the universe of keys

qa_pairs.question: In the comparison model as defined in this lecture, what is the only type of operation an algorithm is allowed to perform to distinguish between items, and what is the consequence of each such operation in terms of branching?
qa_pairs.answer: In the comparison model as defined in this lecture, the only type of operation an algorithm is allowed to perform to distinguish between items is a comparison between keys, specifically operations such as checking whether two keys are equal, whether one is less than another, greater than another, less than or equal, or greater than or equal. The consequence of each such comparison operation is that it can produce only two possible outcomes—true or false—meaning the algorithm branches into exactly two paths at each decision point. This binary branching structure means that any algorithm in the comparison model can be represented as a binary decision tree where internal nodes are comparisons and leaves are output values.

ground_truth_spans.description: Definition of comparison model: only comparisons allowed, each with two possible outputs causing binary branching

qa_pairs.question: The lecture introduces the comparison model using a visual of comparison operators on a blackboard, and later proves a lower bound. What specific operators are shown on the blackboard under 'Comparison Model,' and what lower bound on find(k) does the lecture prove follows from this model?
qa_pairs.answer: The blackboard under 'Comparison Model' shows the operators =, <, >, >=, and <= (and sometimes ≠). From this model, the lecture proves that any correct comparison-based search algorithm requires at least Ω(log n) time to find a key, because a binary decision tree representing the algorithm must have at least n+1 leaves and the minimum height of such a tree is logarithmic.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard caption showing Comparison Model operators =, <, >, >=, <=
Proof that minimum height of binary tree with n+1 leaves is log n, establishing the lower bound

qa_pairs.question: A frame caption shows a blackboard diagram of a 'Direct Access Array' with the label 'X.key=10.' According to the lecture, what is the variable 'u' in the context of this data structure, and what constraint must u satisfy for operations to run in constant time?
qa_pairs.answer: In the context of a direct access array, 'u' is defined as the largest key (i.e., the size of the universe of key space being stored). For operations to run in constant time, u must be less than 2 to the power of w, where w is the word size of the machine — the number of bits the CPU can operate on in constant time.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing Direct Access Array diagram with X.key=10 and label 'U -> largest key'
Explanation that u must be less than 2^w for constant-time operations in the word RAM model

qa_pairs.question: The lecture visually shows the division hash function on the blackboard. What is the formula for the division hash function as written on the blackboard, and why does the lecturer say this function alone is insufficient for guaranteed good performance?
qa_pairs.answer: The blackboard shows the division hash function formula h(k) = k mod m. The lecturer says this function alone is insufficient for guaranteed good performance because it imposes distribution requirements on inputs — if keys are not uniformly distributed over the key space, this function can produce many collisions. Moreover, since u can be large, there always exist inputs that map everything to the same hash index, making worst-case performance bad for any fixed deterministic hash function.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing blackboard with 'Division hash function' and formula h(k) = k mod m
Explanation that fixed hash function cannot guarantee performance independent of inputs when u is large

qa_pairs.question: A frame caption shows the universal hash function formula on the blackboard as h_ab(k) = ((ak+b) mod p) mod m. What are the specific constraints on parameters a and b in the hash family H(p,m) shown on the blackboard, and what property does the lecturer say this family satisfies?
qa_pairs.answer: The blackboard shows that in the hash family H(p,m), parameters a and b are chosen from the range {0, ..., p-1} with the constraint that a ≠ 0. The lecturer states that this family satisfies the universal hashing property: for any two distinct keys k_i and k_j, the probability over a random choice of hash function from H that h(k_i) = h(k_j) is less than or equal to 1/m.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing universal hash function formula h_ab(k) = ((ak+b) mod p) mod m and H(p,m) definition with a,b in {0,...,p-1} and a≠0
Statement of universality property: probability of collision for any two distinct keys is at most 1/m

qa_pairs.question: The lecture uses a decision tree diagram shown on the blackboard to reason about algorithm complexity. According to the visual and the lecture argument, what do the internal nodes and the leaves of this decision tree represent, and how does the number of leaves relate to correctness of a search algorithm?
qa_pairs.answer: According to the lecture and the blackboard diagram, the internal nodes of the decision tree represent comparison operations (branches based on true or false outcomes), while the leaves represent outputs — the points where the algorithm stops and returns a result. For a search algorithm storing n items to be correct, it must have at least n+1 leaves: one for each of the n items it could return, plus one additional leaf for the case where the searched key is not present.

ground_truth_spans.description: Blackboard diagram showing decision tree with internal nodes labeled T/F representing comparisons and leaves representing outputs
Explanation that a correct search algorithm needs at least n+1 leaves to return any of n items or report not found

qa_pairs.question: The lecture explains why a correct comparison-based searching algorithm must have at least n+1 leaves in its decision tree, and later explains how to handle the space problem of direct access arrays. What is the specific reason a correct algorithm needs n+1 leaves, and what variable does the lecture introduce to represent the space problem in direct access arrays?
qa_pairs.answer: A correct comparison-based searching algorithm must have at least n+1 leaves because it needs to be able to return any of the n items it is storing (one leaf per item) plus one additional leaf for the case where the searched key is not found in the data structure. The lecture introduces the variable u to represent the maximum size of any key being stored — the size of the universe of key space — which causes the space problem because a direct access array must allocate space of size u, which can be far larger than n.

ground_truth_spans.description: Explanation that correct algorithm needs at least n+1 leaves to return any of n items or indicate key not found
Introduction of variable u as the maximum key size (universe size) that determines direct access array space requirements

qa_pairs.question: The lecture derives the expected chain length using linearity of expectation and the universality property. What final closed-form expression does the lecture arrive at for the expected size of a chain at index h(k_i), and what condition on m makes this expected chain length constant?
qa_pairs.answer: The lecture derives that the expected size of a chain at h(k_i) equals 1 + (n-1)/m. This is obtained by summing: 1 for the item k_i itself (which always collides with itself) plus the sum of 1/m for each of the other n-1 keys (using the universality property that any two distinct keys collide with probability at most 1/m). The expected chain length becomes constant when m is chosen to be at least order n (i.e., Θ(n)), because (n-1)/m then becomes at most a constant.

ground_truth_spans.description: Application of linearity of expectation and universality property giving expected value 1 + (n-1)/m
Conclusion that choosing m at least order n makes expected chain length constant

qa_pairs.question: According to the lecture, what specific technique is used to make a hash table dynamic as the number of stored items grows, and what analogy does the lecturer draw to justify that this operation does not happen too frequently?
qa_pairs.answer: To make a hash table dynamic, the lecture states that if n grows too large relative to the fixed m (so that m is no longer linear in n), the entire hash table is rebuilt with a new value of m. The lecturer draws an analogy to dynamic arrays, noting that just as with dynamic array resizing, one can prove that this rebuild operation does not happen too often if the table is resized in the right way.

ground_truth_spans.description: Explanation that making hash table dynamic involves rebuilding entire table with new m, analogous to dynamic array resizing

qa_pairs.question: The slide showing the subtree augmentation section lists node.size = node.left.size + node.right.size + 1. How does this equation connect to the lecture's explanation of why a recursive size function would be inefficient?
qa_pairs.answer: The slide equation node.size = node.left.size + node.right.size + 1 represents a stored, precomputed value rather than a live recursive computation. The lecture explains that if size were called as a recursive function, it would take linear time because it would traverse all nodes in the subtree. Instead, by storing the size directly on each node and updating it in O(1) per node by applying this update rule bottom-up through ancestors after any insertion or deletion, the size query becomes O(1) constant time — just return node.size.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing node.size = node.left.size + node.right.size + 1 on the subtree augmentation slide
Explanation that recursive size function gives linear time and why precomputing stored size is needed

qa_pairs.question: The slide on subtree augmentation shows 'subtree property can be computed from properties of node's children (and node) in O(1) time'. How does this relate to the lecture's explanation of why depth cannot be maintained as an augmentation property?
qa_pairs.answer: The slide defines a subtree property as one that can be computed from a node's own children's properties in O(1) time — it is inherently downward-looking within the subtree. Depth, however, depends on how many ancestors a node has above it, which is information from outside the subtree. Specifically, a node's depth depends on the length of the path from the root to that node, which involves nodes not in the node's own subtree. Since depth cannot be computed knowing only the node's children's properties, it violates the subtree property definition and cannot be efficiently maintained as an augmentation.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing subtree augmentation definition: property can be computed from node's children in O(1) time
Explanation that depth is not a subtree property because it depends on nodes outside the subtree

qa_pairs.question: The slide showing the sequence binary tree application lists the subtree_at(node, i) algorithm. How does this algorithm differ from the subtree_find algorithm shown on the set binary tree slide, and why is that difference necessary?
qa_pairs.answer: The set binary tree slide shows subtree_find(node, k) which branches based on comparing a search key k to node.item.key — it uses the actual key values stored in items. The sequence binary tree slide shows subtree_at(node, i) which branches based on comparing index i to nL = size(node.left) — it uses subtree sizes rather than item keys. This difference is necessary because sequence binary trees do not require items to have keys or any ordering property; instead, position is determined structurally by counting nodes in each subtree, allowing arbitrary sequences to be maintained and searched by index.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing subtree_find(node, k) algorithm for set binary tree using key comparisons
Frame caption showing subtree_at(node, i) algorithm for sequence binary tree using nL = size(node.left)

qa_pairs.answer: The slide shows 'Today: Binary Trees II: AVL' as the title, and lists the following topics: subtree augmentation, sequence binary tree via subtree sizes, height balance (AVL) via height augmentation, rotations, rebalancing, and sorting.

qa_pairs.question: How does the lecture's proof that height-balanced AVL trees have O(log n) height use the recurrence for minimum nodes, and what simplification is applied to make the recurrence tractable?
qa_pairs.answer: The lecture constructs the worst-case height-balanced tree where every node has skew of exactly one, giving a recurrence N_h = N_{h-1} + N_{h-2} + 1 for the minimum number of nodes at height h. To make this tractable, the lecture drops the plus-one and replaces N_{h-1} with N_{h-2} — valid because N_{h-1} >= N_{h-2} by induction — yielding N_h >= 2 * N_{h-2}. This recurrence solves to N_h >= 2^(h/2), which is exponential in h, so inverting shows h <= 2 * log n, meaning height is always O(log n), within a factor of two of the ideal.

ground_truth_spans.description: Construction of worst-case AVL tree and statement of recurrence N_h = N_{h-1} + N_{h-2} + 1
Simplification replacing N_{h-1} with N_{h-2} to get N_h >= 2*N_{h-2} and solving to exponential bound

qa_pairs.question: What is the specific update rule for maintaining height as a subtree augmentation in an AVL tree, and why does this make height a valid subtree property?
qa_pairs.answer: The height update rule is: node.height = 1 + max(node.left.height, node.right.height), which computes the height of a node by taking the maximum of the heights of its left and right children and adding one to count the edge to the deeper child. This makes height a valid subtree property because to compute or update the height of any node you need only the already-stored height values of its two direct children — no other information from anywhere else in the tree is needed — and the computation takes O(1) time, satisfying the definition of a subtree property exactly.

ground_truth_spans.description: Explanation of height update rule as max of left and right child heights plus one, and why this is a subtree property

qa_pairs.question: According to the lecture slide shown on screen, what topics are covered under 'Today: Binary Trees II: AVL', and how does the traversal order concept introduced in the recall section connect to the sequence binary tree application discussed later?
qa_pairs.answer: The slide lists six topics under 'Today: Binary Trees II: AVL': subtree augmentation, sequence binary tree via subtree sizes, height balance (AVL) via height augmentation, rotations, rebalancing, and sorting. The recall section defines traversal order as recursively traversing the left subtree, then outputting the root, then traversing the right subtree. This traversal order directly defines the sequence order maintained by a sequence binary tree, meaning the traversal order of the tree is set to equal the sequence order the programmer wants to represent.

ground_truth_spans.description: Slide caption listing today's topics including subtree augmentation, sequence binary tree via subtree sizes, height balance AVL, rotations, rebalancing, sorting
Explanation that sequence binary tree sets traversal order equal to sequence order

qa_pairs.question: The lecture slide shows the subtree_find algorithm for a set binary tree, and the spoken explanation connects it to binary search on arrays. What is the BST property shown on the slide, and how does the lecturer describe the relationship between subtree_find and binary search on arrays?
qa_pairs.answer: The slide shows the BST property as: keys in subtree(node.left) < node.item.key < keys in subtree(node.right). The lecturer explains that this algorithm is exactly binary search on an array: binary search first looks at the key in the middle (drawn as the root), then recurses either on the left chunk or the right chunk, so a perfect binary tree simulates exactly binary search in an array. The lecturer calls set binary trees 'binary search trees' because they are the tree version of binary search.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing BST property and subtree_find algorithm on slide
Lecturer explains subtree_find generalizes binary search from arrays to trees, calls set binary trees binary search trees

qa_pairs.question: The frame caption for the subtree augmentation section shows the equation node.size = node.left.size + node.right.size + 1. How does the lecturer justify storing this value on every node rather than computing it recursively, and how many ancestor nodes must be updated when a leaf is added?
qa_pairs.answer: The lecturer explains that computing size recursively as a function call would give a linear-time algorithm, which would make the overall operations very slow. Instead, size is stored on every single node and precomputed, so the function simply returns node.size in constant time. When a leaf is added, only the ancestors of that new node need their size updated, which the lecturer says is order H ancestors (approximately exactly H), since subtrees not containing the new leaf are unchanged.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecturer explains storing size on every node rather than recursive computation, frame caption shows node.size equation
Lecturer explains that adding a leaf requires updating order H ancestors up the tree

qa_pairs.question: The frame captions show a slide listing 'subtree augmentation' with the rule that each node can store O(1) extra fields, and later a slide showing the subtree_at algorithm. What index adjustment does the subtree_at algorithm make when recursing into the right subtree, and why is this necessary according to the lecture?
qa_pairs.answer: When recursing into the right subtree, the subtree_at algorithm subtracts nL and 1 from the index i, computing i minus nL minus 1. This is necessary because the numbering system changes when moving into the right subtree: in the global tree, index zero is the leftmost node, but within the right subtree, index zero refers to the first node of that subtree. The root node itself occupies index nL, so the right subtree's local index must subtract both nL (the left subtree count) and 1 (for the root node).

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows subtree_at algorithm with nL computation and recursion cases
Lecturer explains the subtraction i-nL-1 when recursing right, because numbering resets in the right subtree

qa_pairs.question: A frame caption shows the AVL height balance condition as height(node.right) - height(node.left) in {-1, 0, +1}. The lecturer then proves that height-balanced trees have logarithmic height using a recurrence. What recurrence does the lecturer set up and what is the final bound on height H in terms of n?
qa_pairs.answer: The lecturer defines N_h as the minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree of height H. In the worst case, one subtree has height H-1 and the other has height H-2, giving the recurrence N_h = N_{h-1} + N_{h-2} + 1. The lecturer then lower-bounds this by replacing N_{h-1} with N_{h-2} and dropping the +1, giving N_h ≥ 2 * N_{h-2}, which solves to approximately 2^(h/2). Inverting this exponential relationship gives H ≤ 2 * log n, so AVL trees always have height at most 2 log n.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption shows AVL height balance condition skew in {-1,0,+1} and lecturer sets up recurrence N_h = N_{h-1} + N_{h-2} + 1
Lecturer solves recurrence to show N_h is exponential and concludes H is at most 2 log n

qa_pairs.question: The frame caption for the subtree augmentation section shows 'each node can store O(1) extra fields/properties' and the height update rule. How does the lecturer use height as a subtree property, and what happens to the augmentation of nodes X and Y specifically after a rotation is performed?
qa_pairs.answer: Height is a subtree property because the height of a node equals the maximum of the heights of its left and right children plus one, which can be computed in constant time from the children's stored heights. After a rotation, the subtrees A, B, and C do not change, so their augmentations are unaffected. However, X's subtree changes because X now has Y as a child (or vice versa), so X's augmentation must be updated. Then Y's augmentation must also be updated. Finally, all ancestors of X must be updated walking up the tree.

ground_truth_spans.description: Lecturer explains height is a subtree property computed as max of children heights plus one
Lecturer explains after rotation X and Y augmentations must be updated, then all ancestors

qa_pairs.answer: The frame caption shows the function subtree_at(node, i) with the following steps: nL = size(node.left); if i < nL, call Subtree_at(node.left, i); if i = nL, return node; if i > nL, call Subtree_at(node.right, i - nL). qa_pairs.question: Why can the global index of a node not be maintained as a subtree augmentation property, and why can depth also not be maintained as a subtree property according to the lecture?
qa_pairs.answer: The global index cannot be maintained as a subtree property because if a new node is inserted at the beginning of the traversal order, every node's index changes. This means one change causes all properties to change simultaneously, which is an extreme example of a global property rather than a local one. The lecturer notes that a node's index depends on how many nodes are to its left (its predecessors), which are not in the subtree of that node. Depth is also mentioned as annoying to maintain, though the lecturer says it is not obvious why at that moment.

Lecturer explains index depends on nodes to the left which are outside the node's subtree, and mentions depth is also not maintainable

qa_pairs.question: What property of a simple graph is displayed in large text on the lecture slide, and how does the instructor derive the specific bound for the directed case using the binomial coefficient?
qa_pairs.answer: The lecture slide displays the property that |E| = O(|V|²) in large text. For the directed case, the instructor derives that |E| ≤ 2·C(n,2), because any unique pair of vertices can be chosen (captured by the binomial coefficient C(n,2)), but one must multiply by two to account for the fact that an edge from V to W is distinct from an edge from W to V in a directed graph. Expanding the binomial coefficient gives n(n-1)/2, and multiplying by two yields O(n²).

qa_pairs.question: How does the instructor justify that the sum of degrees over all vertices equals 2|E| for undirected graphs, and how does the analogous formula differ for directed graphs when only counting outgoing degree?
qa_pairs.answer: For undirected graphs, the instructor argues that every edge is simultaneously ingoing and outgoing at both its endpoints, so each edge contributes 1 to the degree of two different vertices. Summing degrees over all vertices therefore counts each edge twice, giving Σ deg(v) = 2|E|. For directed graphs, if one counts only the outgoing degree of each vertex, each directed edge (u,v) contributes exactly once to the out-degree of u and not at all to the out-degree of v. Summing outgoing degrees over all vertices therefore counts each edge exactly once, giving Σ deg⁺(v) = |E|, with a coefficient of 1 rather than 2.

ground_truth_spans.description: Instructor explains undirected case: each edge contributes to degree of two vertices so sum = 2|E|
Instructor explains directed case: counting only outgoing degree gives coefficient 1, so sum = |E|

qa_pairs.question: What is the shortest path tree, why does it only require linear space, and how does the instructor use the subpath optimality property of shortest paths to justify this construction?
qa_pairs.answer: The shortest path tree is a data structure that, for every vertex v, stores only P(v)—the single predecessor vertex on the shortest path from the source s to v—rather than storing the entire path. It requires only O(|V|) space because each vertex stores exactly one pointer. The instructor justifies this by the subpath optimality property of shortest paths: if the shortest path from s to d passes through c, then the portion from s to c must itself be a shortest path to c. If it were not, one could splice in a shorter path to c and thereby obtain a shorter path to d, contradicting the assumption. Therefore, the full path to any vertex can always be reconstructed by repeatedly following P(v) back to s, and no additional information needs to be stored.

ground_truth_spans.description: Instructor argues shortest paths have subpath optimality: prefix of shortest path to d is shortest path to intermediate vertex c
Instructor defines P(v) as predecessor, argues storing one pointer per vertex gives linear space and suffices to reconstruct paths

qa_pairs.question: In the context of this lecture, what specific real-world application from the instructor's own research does he give to illustrate how adding a single edge to a graph can change the shortest-path distances to every vertex?
qa_pairs.answer: The instructor describes his work with 3D models of ballerinas. He explains that ballerinas are problematic because sometimes they put their hands together, and when that happens, adding this contact edge suddenly changes the shortest path between the fingers from a long path through the entire body down to essentially zero distance. This means that incremental shortest-path algorithms can fail catastrophically in this scenario because adding one edge forces recomputation of shortest paths to virtually every vertex in the mesh graph.

ground_truth_spans.description: Instructor describes 3D ballerina models: hands touching creates new edge that changes shortest paths from full-body length to near zero

The instructor explains that with an edge list, checking for an edge requires scanning all edges, taking time proportional to the number of edges, which could be up to O(V²). By contrast, an adjacency list that stores Adj(u) as a hash table allows an expected O(1) lookup for the vertex u and another O(1) lookup within the hash table, reducing edge-existence checking to expected O(1) time.

Instructor explains adjacency list with hash table gives O(1) expected edge lookup

The instructor explains that BFS computes all these level sets incrementally—level set i is built from level set i−1—and that this gives the shortest distances and the shortest path tree. He states L_0 must contain only the source vertex s because the only vertex at distance zero from the source is the source itself.

Instructor explains BFS computes level sets and initializes L_0={s} because source is distance 0 from itself

qa_pairs.question: How does the instructor justify that the sum of degrees over all vertices equals 2|E| for undirected graphs, and how does that formula then help bound the runtime of algorithms that iterate over all vertices and their neighbors?
qa_pairs.answer: The instructor justifies the formula by noting that in an undirected graph every edge is adjacent to exactly two vertices, so when you sum up the degrees of all vertices each edge is counted twice, giving the sum of deg(v) for all v in V equals 2|E|. For directed graphs, counting only outgoing degrees means each edge is counted once, so the sum equals |E|. This formula is then directly applied to bound the runtime of nested loops—iterating over every vertex and then over every neighbor—showing the total work is proportional to the number of edges, which combined with the initialization cost gives an overall O(|V|+|E|) runtime for BFS.

ground_truth_spans.description: Instructor proves sum of deg(v)=2|E| for undirected by edge double-counting argument
Instructor references that formula to bound BFS runtime as O(V+E)

qa_pairs.question: Why does the instructor prefer an algorithm with runtime O(|V|+|E|) over one expressed solely as O(|V|²), even though both can describe the same worst-case bound for simple graphs?
qa_pairs.answer: The instructor prefers O(|V|+|E|) because many real-world graphs are sparse—meaning they have far fewer edges than the maximum possible |V|² edges—so an algorithm scaling with |E| is much faster in practice than one scaling with |V|². The expression O(|V|+|E|) is more informative because it captures both the initialization cost proportional to vertices and the iteration cost proportional to edges separately; if the graph has no edges the vertex term dominates, while as edges grow the edge term can approach |V|². Simply saying O(|V|²) loses this distinction and overstates the cost for sparse graphs.

ground_truth_spans.description: Instructor explains sparse graphs make O(E) algorithms preferable to O(V²) algorithms
Instructor argues O(V+E) is more informative than O(V²) for BFS runtime

qa_pairs.question: According to the lecture, what is the specific coding bug the instructor warns about when computing the length of a path?
qa_pairs.answer: The instructor warns that a very common coding bug is accidentally adding one to the path length. He explains that the length of a path is the number of edges traversed, which equals the number of vertices in the path minus one, and that programmers frequently add one by mistake because there is always one more vertex than edges in a path.

ground_truth_spans.description: Instructor warns about off-by-one bug: path length is number of edges, one fewer than number of vertices

qa_pairs.question: Based on the frame captions showing the board and the instructor's explanation, what is the formal definition of the shortest path distance delta(s,t) when no path exists, and what additional case must be accounted for in weighted graphs that did not arise in BFS?
qa_pairs.answer: When no path exists from s to t, by convention delta(s,t) is set to positive infinity. The additional case unique to weighted graphs is negative-weight cycles: if there exists a path from s to v that goes through a vertex on a negative weight cycle, then delta(s,v) is set to negative infinity, because one can traverse the cycle arbitrarily many times to make the path weight arbitrarily small.

ground_truth_spans.description: Board shows delta(s,t) = inf{w(pi) | path pi from s to t} and if no path delta(s,t) = infinity
Board and instructor explain negative-weight cycles cause delta(s,v) = -infinity

qa_pairs.question: The board shows that BFS was listed as the SSSP algorithm for unweighted graphs in the restrictions table. The lecture also discusses a reduction from positive-weight graphs to BFS. What condition must hold for the total weights to make this BFS reduction run in linear time?
qa_pairs.answer: For the BFS reduction to achieve linear time, the sum of all edge weights in the graph must be asymptotically less than V plus E (i.e., the total weight is O(V+E)). In this reduction, each edge of weight w is replaced by w unweighted edges in series, so the total number of new edges must remain linear for BFS to run in O(V+E) time overall.

ground_truth_spans.description: Frame caption showing BFS in the restrictions table with O(|V|+|E|) running time for unweighted/general graphs
Instructor explains that if sum of all weights is asymptotically less than V+E, the BFS reduction gives linear time

qa_pairs.question: The board shows a shortest-path trees algorithm with pseudocode. What condition is checked before setting P(v) = u in that algorithm, and why does the lecture say we only need parent pointers for vertices with finite shortest path distance?
qa_pairs.answer: The condition checked is that v is not already in P (no parent pointer assigned yet) AND that delta(s,v) equals delta(s,u) plus w(u,v), meaning the edge (u,v) lies on a shortest path. The lecture explains we only need parent pointers for vertices with finite shortest path distances because vertices with infinite distance are unreachable from the source, and vertices with negative-infinity distance (on negative-weight cycles) have no finite-length shortest path, so parent pointers are meaningless for them.

ground_truth_spans.description: Board caption shows pseudocode: if v not in P and delta(s,v) = delta(s,u) + w(u,v), set P(v) = u
Instructor explains only need P(v) for v with finite delta(s,v), not for infinite or minus-infinite cases
